{{Short description|Big Five traits across cultures}}
The Big Five personality [[Trait theory|traits]] are [[Openness to experience|Openness]], [[Conscientiousness]], [[Extraversion and introversion|Extraversion]], [[Agreeableness]], and [[Neuroticism]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Schmitt |first1=David P. |last2=Allik |first2=Jüri |last3=McCrae |first3=Robert R. |last4=Benet-Martínez |first4=Verónica |date=2007 |title=The Geographic Distribution of Big Five Personality Traits |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0022022106297299 |journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology |volume=38 |issue=2 |pages=173–212 |doi=10.1177/0022022106297299 |hdl=20.500.12724/2395 |s2cid=86619840 |issn=0022-0221|hdl-access=free }}</ref> The Big Five Personality is a test that people can take to learn more about their personality in relation to the five personality traits.<ref name=":0" /> [[Cross-cultural psychology]] as a discipline examines the way that human behavior is different and/or similar across different [[culture]]s. One important and widely studied area in this subfield of [[psychology]] is [[personality psychology|personality]], particularly the study of [[Big Five personality traits|Big Five]].<ref name=":0" /> The Big Five model of personality (also known as the Five Factor Model) has become the most extensively studied model of personality and has broad support, starting in the United States and later in many different cultures.<ref name="John1999">{{cite book |last1=John |first1=Oliver P |last2=Srivastava |first2=Sanjay |editor1-last=Pervin |editor1-first=L A |editor2-last=John |editor2-first=Oliver P |title=Handbook of personality: theory and research |date=1999 |publisher=Guilford Press |isbn=978-1-57230-695-0 |pages=102–138 |edition=2nd |url=https://pages.uoregon.edu/sanjay/pubs/bigfive.pdf |chapter=The Big Five Trait taxonomy: History, measurement, and theoretical perspectives}}</ref> The Big Five model of personality (also known as the Five Factor Model or the Big Five Inventory) started in the United States, and through the years has been translated into many languages and has been used in many countries.<ref name=":0" /> Some researchers were attempting to determine the differences in how other cultures perceive this model.<ref name=":0" /> Some research shows that the Big Five holds up across cultures even with its origin in the English language.<ref>{{Citation|last=Rolland|first=Jean-Pierre|title=The Cross-Cultural Generalizability of the Five-Factor Model of Personality|date=2002|work=The Five-Factor Model of Personality Across Cultures|pages=7–28|publisher=Springer US|doi=10.1007/978-1-4615-0763-5_2|isbn=978-0-306-47355-5}}</ref>  However, there is also some evidence which suggests that the Big Five traits may not be sufficient to completely explain personality in other cultures.<ref name="funder" /> In countries such as South America and East Asia, the results weren't as accurate because they weren't as open as some people in other countries are.<ref name=":0" />

==Supportive research==

Research suggests that the same five-factor structure of personality can be found in multiple other countries, based on a translated version of the [[Revised NEO Personality Inventory]].<ref name="mccraecosta">{{Cite journal 
 |author1=McCrae, R. R.  |author2=Costa, P. T.   |name-list-style=amp |date=May 1997
 | title = Personality trait structure as a human universal.
 | journal = [[American Psychologist]]
 | volume = 52 | issue = 2 | pages = 509–516
 | doi = 10.1037/0003-066X.52.5.509
|pmid=9145021 |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1231466 }}</ref> Studies on the validity of the Five-Factor Model using translations of the [[Revised NEO Personality Inventory]] have found broad support across many studies and in many countries; in earlier studies, Extraversion and Neuroticism were reported as stable personality scales across several cultures, including German, Dutch, French, Japanese, Chinese, and Filipino samples.<ref name="mccrae1998">{{Cite journal 
 |author1=McCrae, R. R. |author2=Costa, P. T. |author3=Del Pilar, G. H. |author4=Rolland, J. P. |author5=Parker, W. D. |date=January 1998
 | title = Cross-cultural assessment of the five-factor model: The revised NEO personality inventory.
 | journal = [[Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology]]
 | volume = 29 | issue = 1 | pages = 171–188
 | doi = 10.1177/0022022198291009
|s2cid=145375858 |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1235534 }}</ref>

Further research found support for the entire Five-Factor Model in Chinese, Dutch, Italian, Hungarian, German, Australian, South African, Canadian, Finnish, Polish, Portuguese, Israeli, Korean, Japanese, and Filipino samples, in addition to other samples.<ref name="mccrae1998" /> Across multiple studies, [[Factor analysis|factor analyses]] of translations of the [[Revised NEO Personality Inventory]] in languages from different [[language families]] consistently load on five factors that largely correspond to the Big Five personality traits.<ref name="mccrae1998" />  Additionally, the Big Five traits have been found in the personality ratings of observers in over 50 cultures, indicating that the previous findings were not dependent merely on ratings of the self.<ref>{{Cite journal 
 |author=McCrae, R. R. |author2=Terracciano, A. |collaboration=Personality Profiles of Cultures Project |date=March 2005
 | title = Universal features of personality trait terms from the observer's perspective: Data from 50 cultures.
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | volume = 88 | issue = 3 | pages = 29–44
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.88.3.547
|pmid=15740445
 |url=http://repository.ust.hk/ir/bitstream/1783.1-2007/1/McCrae05.pdf
 }}</ref>  Overall, this body of work has established the validity of the Five-Factor model cross-culturally, potentially providing evidence for the Five-Factor Model as a universal taxonomy of personality structure.<ref name="mccraecosta" />

==Comparisons==

One approach psychologists have taken when examining Big Five traits in different cultures has been to examine either similarities or differences between cultures.  Generally, researchers examine the average levels of a trait (or multiple traits) across an entire culture to make comparisons cross-culturally.<ref name="matsumoto">{{cite book|last=Matsumoto|first=David|title=Culture & Psychology|year=2008|publisher=Thomson & Wadsworth|location=CA|isbn=978-0-495-09787-7|author2=Linda Juang}}</ref>

===Similarities===
There are many similarities in Big Five trait expression across cultures. For example, differences between men and women in Big Five traits, although small compared to variation within gender, do seem to exist consistently across a number of cultures. In general, women tend to score higher on neuroticism and agreeableness.<ref name="costa2001">{{Cite journal
 |author1=Costa, P.T. |author2=Terracciano, A. |author3=McCrae, R. R. |date=August 2001
 | title = Gender differences in personality traits across cultures: Robust and surprising findings.
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | volume = 81 | issue = 2 | pages = 322–331
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.81.2.322
 | pmid=11519935
}}</ref> Additionally, longitudinal studies have found consistency in personality changes that occur across the lifetime, in both adults and adolescents.<ref name="adults">{{Cite journal
 |author=McCrae, R. R. |author2=Costa, P. T. |author3=de Lima, M. P. |author4=Simões, A. |author5=Ostendorf, F. |author6=Angleitner, A. |author7=Marušić, I. |author8=Bratko, D. |author9=Caprara, G. V. |author10=Barbaranelli, C. |author11=Chae, J. |author12=Piedmont, R. L.
 |date=March 1999
 | title = Age differences in personality across the adult life span: Parallels in five cultures.
 |journal = [[Developmental Psychology (journal)|Developmental Psychology]]
 | volume = 35 | issue = 2 | pages = 466–477
 | doi = 10.1037/0012-1649.35.2.466
 | pmid=10082017
}}</ref><ref name="teens">{{Cite journal
 |author=McCrae, R. R. |author2=Costa, P. T. |author3=de Lima, M. P. |author4=Simões, A. |author5=Ostendorf, F. |author6=Angleitner, A. |author7=Marušić, I. |author8=Bratko, D. |author9=Caprara, G. V. |author10=Barbaranelli, C. |author11=Chae, J. |author12=Piedmont, R. L.
 |date=December 2002
 | title = Personality trait development from age 12 to age 18: Longitudinal, cross-sectional and cross-cultural analyses.
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | volume = 83 | issue = 6 | pages = 1456–1468
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.6.1456
 | pmid=12500824
}}</ref> Research in Big Five traits in American and [[Flemish people|Flemish]] teens showed similar changes in personality from ages 12 to 18.<ref name="teens" /> In addition, the period from young adulthood to middle adulthood is associated with increases in Conscientiousness and Agreeableness and decreases in Neuroticism, Openness, and Extraversion in several countries, including the United States, Germany, Italy, Portugal, Croatia, and South Korea.<ref name="adults" />

===Differences===
It is also important to note that when examining the average personality traits of individuals in cultural groups, differences between cultures seem to exist.<ref name="McCrae1">{{Cite journal 
 |author=McCrae, R. R. |author2=Terracciano, A. |collaboration=Personality Profiles of Cultures Project |date=September 2005
 | title = Personality profiles of cultures: Aggregate personality traits. 
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | volume = 89 | issue = 3 | pages = 407–425
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.407 
 | pmid=16248722
|url=http://idiprints.knjiznica.idi.hr/649/1/JoPaSP%202005_3%20Mccrae%20et%20al..pdf
 }}</ref>  Some research compares one culture against another culture on a specific Big Five personality trait; Filipinos, for example, score relatively low on Neuroticism on average, compared to other cultures measured, while scoring in the middle of the scale on Extraversion.<ref name="McCrae1" />  Americans, New Zealanders, and Canadians score higher on Extraversion, while scoring moderately on Neuroticism.<ref name="McCrae1" />  These differences, however, exist on average, and there is still a large amount of variability in Big Five personality traits that exists within a particular culture.<ref name="McCrae1" /> Gender differences in personality tend to be larger in developed societies (such as France and the United States) compared to less-developed countries (such as Zimbabwe and Malaysia).<ref name="funder">{{cite book|last=Funder|first=David|title=The Personality Puzzle|year=2010|isbn=978-0-393-93348-2|publisher=WW Norton & Company|location=NY}}</ref><ref name="costa2001" /> However, although these findings are quite robust, one consideration is that these differences between cultures might be the result of translation errors, differences in self-presentation styles, or even genetic differences.<ref name="funder" /><ref name="mccrae1998" />

Furthermore, although broad evidence suggests that the Big Five traits do measure meaningful constructs across a great deal of cultures, it is also true that expressions of mean levels of personality are necessarily influenced by culture.<ref name="mccrae1998" />  That is to say, all individuals scoring high on a certain Big Five personality trait such as Extraversion are likely to enjoy socialization with others, but where, when, and with whom they socialize is necessarily influenced by their cultural milieu.<ref name="mccrae1998" />  Thus, it may be most productive to think of the Five Factor Model as a framework for beginning to explore systematically individual differences in behavior within a particular culture.<ref name="mccrae1998" />

==Criticism==
Some controversy exists over whether or not the Big Five are relevant to all other cultures, especially given that the Big Five were developed via factor analysis from English words.<ref name=":24">{{Cite journal |last=Becker |first=Peter |date=1999 |title=Beyond the big five  |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=26 |issue=3 |pages=511–530|doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(98)00168-8 }}</ref> The factor analysis helped determine that there are only five factors.<ref name=":24" /> Although support for the Big Five across cultures is quite robust, it is unclear whether or not the Big Five personality traits are the best possible measure of personality across all cultures.<ref name=":0" /> Another model that can measure personality is the [[Myers–Briggs Type Indicator]].<ref name=":46">{{Cite journal |last1=Hook |first1=Joshua N. |last2=Hall |first2=Todd W. |last3=Davis |first3=Don E. |last4=Van Tongeren |first4=Daryl R. |last5=Conner |first5=Mackenzie |date=2020-12-17 |title=The Enneagram: A systematic review of the literature and directions for future research |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jclp.23097?saml_referrer |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=77 |issue=4 |pages=865–883 |doi=10.1002/jclp.23097 |pmid=33332604 |s2cid=229316947 |issn=0021-9762|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Some additional controversy to the Five Factor Model is that this model can measure up to relating to personalities which implies that most people think this is the best model.<ref name=":14">{{cite book |last=McCrae |first=Robert |editor1-first=Philip J |editor1-last=Corr |editor2-first=Gerald |editor2-last=Matthews |date=2009 |chapter=The Five-Factor Model of personality traits: consensus and controversy |url=https://nibmehub.com/opac-service/pdf/read/Personality%20Psychology.pdf#page=204 |title=The Cambridge Handbook of Personality Psychology |pages=148–161|doi=10.1017/CBO9780511596544.012 |isbn=978-0-511-59654-4 }}</ref> One problem that some people, specifically psychologists, have with the personality traits is how accurate they are within other cultures.<ref name=":0" /> Some researchers suggest that important aspects of certain cultures which could include the personality traits of honesty and humility are not captured by the Five Factor Model.<ref name=":24" /><ref name=":14" /> The Five Factor Model is used in several cultures throughout North America, South America, Europe, Africa, and Asia.<ref name=":0" />

Throughout the years, the Five Factor Model has experienced many changes as it has developed because it originally only had three factors within Eysenck's model which only included Extraversion, Psychoticism, and Neuroticism.<ref name=":14" /> Some parts of the Five Factor Model have been challenged or changed by other researchers based on their prior knowledge.<ref name=":14" /> Some researchers tend to question the personality traits included in this because some believe that the traits should be more specific.<ref name=":14" /> Most of the controversy with the Five Factor Model involves the traits and personality, as well as the origin of these five factors.<ref name=":32">{{Cite journal |last1=Strus |first1=W. |last2=Cieciuch |first2=J. |date=2014 |title=The circumplex of personality metatraits: A synthesizing model of personality based on the big five |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1037/gpr0000017 |journal=Review of General Psychology |volume=18 |issue=4 |pages=273–286|doi=10.1037/gpr0000017 |s2cid=147445246 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Some researchers don't believe that the Five Factor Model uses personality correctly.<ref name=":32" />

Several questions that researchers have asked about controversy about the Five Factor Model is if people can understand it, if the measures are accurate, and if it represents personality correctly.<ref name=":24" /> The measures for the Five Factor Model include the Big Five Inventory which has 44 items to measure the five personality traits.<ref name=":0" /> The Five Factor Model is questioned if people can understand it because there is controversy if more personality traits should be included in this model or not.<ref name=":24" /> Some researchers question if the measures are accurate because of the variables that are used and the bias that is often found within this model.<ref name=":24" /> There are even some researchers that want to study if the Five Factor Model represents personality correctly, since some researchers believe that there should be more or fewer categories of personality traits involved.<ref name=":24" />

Beyond the validity of the factor structure across cultures, there has been criticism of the idea that it can be used to measure cultural differences. Researchers compared self-report personality data for conscientiousness across cultures in Europe and compared these to behavioral markers of conscientiousness across cultures, such as the accuracy of bank clocks and how efficiently post office workers gave change for a purchase.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Heine |first=Steven J. |last2=Buchtel |first2=Emma E. |last3=Norenzayan |first3=Ara |date=2008-04-01 |title=What Do Cross-National Comparisons of Personality Traits Tell Us?: The Case of Conscientiousness |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02085.x |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=19 |issue=4 |pages=309–313 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02085.x |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref> For most markers, personality measures of conscientiousness were correlated in the wrong direction with the behavioral markers across cultures. For example, cultures that had more accurate bank clocks tended to score lower on conscientiousness in the Big Five personality scale. One explanation for this is the "reference group effect," whereby people in very conscientious cultures rate themselves lower because they perceive the people around them as very conscientious.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Heine |first=Steven J. |last2=Lehman |first2=Darrin R. |last3=Peng |first3=Kaiping |last4=Greenholtz |first4=Joe |date=June 2002 |title=What's wrong with cross-cultural comparisons of subjective Likert scales?: The reference-group effect. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.82.6.903 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=82 |issue=6 |pages=903–918 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.6.903 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== HEXACO model of personality ===
{{Main|HEXACO model of personality structure}}
One proposed alternative to the Big Five that has been developed via cross-cultural research is the HEXACO model.<ref name="Ashton1">{{Cite journal |author1=Ashton, M.C. |author2=Lee, K. |author3=Perugini, M. |author4=Szarota, P. |author5=de Vries, R. E. |author6=Di Blas, L. |author7=Boies, K. |author8=De Raad, B. |date=February 2004 |title=A Six-Factor Structure of Personality-Descriptive Adjectives: Solutions From Psycholexical Studies in Seven Languages. |journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] |volume=86 |issue=2 |pages=356–366 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.2.356 |pmid=14769090}}</ref>  This model builds on the research of the Big Five traits, with the novel addition of a trait named [[Honesty-humility facet of the HEXACO model of personality|Honesty-Humility]].<ref name="Ashton2">{{Cite journal
 |author1=Ashton, M.C.  |author2=Lee, K.  |name-list-style=amp |date=October 2005
 | title = Honesty-Humility, the Big Five, and the Five-Factor Model.
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality]]
 | volume = 73 | issue = 5 | pages = 1321–1354
 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-6494.2005.00351.x | pmid=16138875
}}</ref>  Individuals high in the trait of honesty-humility are associated with the characteristics of straightforwardness, modesty, and fairness.<ref name="Ashton2" />  In addition, the HEXACO model contains slightly rotated versions of two of the Big Five traits (Agreeableness and Neuroticism) such that sentimentality/toughness becomes part of the old Neuroticism trait (and renamed Emotionality) and anger/even-temper becomes associated with the new Agreeableness trait.<ref name="Ashton2" />  This rotation creates less overlap among the six personality traits of the HEXACO, and allows for better prediction of behaviors such as deceit without hostility (e.g. social monitoring).<ref name="Ashton2" />  Support for the HEXACO model has been found in multiple countries, including Dutch, French, German, Italian, Korean, Polish, and English samples.<ref name="Ashton2" />

===Chinese Personality Assessment Inventory===
Chinese psychologists have attempted to develop an indigenous measure of personality, named the Chinese Personality Assessment Inventory (CPAI).<ref name="funder" />  Traits in the CPAI model have also collectively been referred to as "Interpersonal Relatedness," and include:

* Harmony (one's inner peace of mind, interpersonal harmony, etc.)
* ''Ren Qing'' (relationship orientation, which covers adherence to cultural norms of interaction such as those based on reciprocity)
* Modernization (contrasted with attitudes toward traditional Chinese beliefs)
* Thrift vs. Extravagance
* ''Ah-Q'' Mentality (defensiveness, externalization of blame)
* Face (social behaviors done in order to enhance or avoid damaging one's reputation and honor)<ref name="matsumoto" /><ref name="cheung">{{cite journal|last=Cheung|first=F. M.|author2=Leung, K.|author3=Fan, R. M.|author4=Song, W.S.|author5=Zhang, J. X.|author6=Zhang, H. P.|title=Development of the Chinese Personality Assessment Inventory.|journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology|date=March 1996|volume=27|issue=2|pages=181–199|doi=10.1177/0022022196272003|s2cid=145134209}}</ref>

Support for this model of personality was originally developed in studies in mainland China and Hong Kong, China, but the existence of the Interpersonal Relatedness dimension of personality has also been found in samples from Singapore, Hawaii, and the Midwestern United States.<ref name="matsumoto" />

===Other possible models===
Other researchers have found different personality dimensions that may exists in different cultural contexts.<ref name="matsumoto" />  For example, one study of a Filipino sample used both indigenous Filipino personality scales and the [[Revised NEO Personality Inventory|NEO-PI-R]], and although there was overlap between the Filipino scales and the Five Factor Model, researchers also found indigenous factors such as Pagkamadaldal (Social Curiosity) and Pagkamapagsapalaran (Risk-Taking) that had predictive power greater than the Five Factor Model alone.<ref name="matsumoto" /><ref name=katigbak>{{cite journal|last=Katigbak|first=M. S. |author2=Church, A. T. |author3=Guanzon-Lapeña, M. A. |author4=Carlota, A. J. |author5=del Pilar, G. H. |title=Are indigenous personality dimensions culture specific? Philippine inventories and the five-factor model.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=January 2002|volume=82|issue=1|pages=89–101|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.1.89|pmid=11811638}}</ref>  Other research using indigenous approaches to traits has taken place in countries such as India, Korea, and Greece.<ref name="matsumoto" />  A Chinese factor analysis of traits in 2009 found seven factors (three or four of which resembled Big Five traits). A similar study in Spain in 1997 found seven Spanish personality factors. However, the seven factors were not the same across the two countries.<ref name="funder" />  Thus, it is clear that although there is strong support for the Big Five across cultures, some research suggests the existence of other traits besides simply the Big Five, which may ultimately improve our understanding of personality across different cultures.

==References==
